title: Google I/O 2025 – 3zXVPU2vKXs
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] DONOVAN MCMURRAY: Hi,
I'm Donovan McMurray and today I'll be joined by my
colleagues, Kristina and Mayuri. We're going to share how
to create seamless video capture, editing, and playback
with CameraX and Media3. As a bonus, we'll see how to
add custom effects each step of the way. Let's get started. Billions of users have chosen
Android as the platform where they express themselves
and connect with others. More and more, these
users are looking for new, innovative, and fun
ways to express themselves. Today, we'll focus on
using our Jetpack CameraX, and Media3 libraries to build
delightful video creation and consumption experiences. To start things off,
I'll cover CameraX for easy-to-implement camera
integration into your app. Then Kristina will talk
about Media3 Transformer, which handles video editing,
transcoding, and more. Finally, Mayuri will go over
Media3 ExoPlayer and the options you can set for video playback. Jetpack libraries are
flexible, performant, reliable, and consistent, and an
increasing number of apps are using CameraX
in Media3 every day. In fact, nearly half of
the most popular apps are already using these Jetpack
libraries for their camera and media needs. So let's dive in and see what
these libraries are capable of. We'll look at a number
of code examples from one of our hero
sample apps, Socialite, which can be found on
our GitHub page here. Let's start by using
CameraX to capture some videos with effects. We first need to set up
the basic video recording. A quick recap of
CameraX's structure-- you select which
use cases your app needs when setting up CameraX. These use cases are preview,
image capture, video capture, and image analysis. For our app, we
only need preview to show the user
what's being captured and video capture to
save a video file. First, let's create
the preview use case. We'll set our aspect ratio
strategy to be a 16-by-9 ratio. CameraX also supports
a 4-by-3 ratio. Then we create a
resolution selector and set the aspect
ratio strategy.

title: Google I/O 2025 – 3zXVPU2vKXs
text:
First, let's create
the preview use case. We'll set our aspect ratio
strategy to be a 16-by-9 ratio. CameraX also supports
a 4-by-3 ratio. Then we create a
resolution selector and set the aspect
ratio strategy. There are other options we could
set, like whether to prioritize capture rate or resolution. We'll use the default and
prioritize capture rate for a smooth-looking preview. Then we create the preview use
case using a builder, like so. To create the video
capture use case, we first need to
create a recorder. Let's set the aspect ratio
to match our preview. And for the quality,
we'll choose "highest" to let CameraX find the best
quality supported on a given device. You can specify qualities,
like standard definition, high definition,
full high definition, and ultra-high definition. But not all of them are
supported on every device, so you also need to specify a
fallback strategy in that case. Then we use the recorder to
create a video capture use case. To move things
along, I won't show all the details of setting
up the video output file. Check out the CameraX
foundations video series in this video's description
for that full explanation. And with that, we're close to
having a working video recording app already. All we need to do to finish
setting up the camera is to create a camera
provider with CameraX. The awaitInstance method
is a suspending function that returns a fully
initialized camera provider. Then we'll create a
use case group builder and add our preview and
video capture use cases. One last thing-- before we
bind these new use cases, we need to make sure any
previously bound use cases are unbound. Then we call bindToLifecycle
with a lifecycle owner, a camera selector--
in this case, we'll use the
default back camera-- and our use case group,
which we build inline here. And that's all you need to set
up video recording in your app. Now, I started off
this talk by saying users are looking for new,
innovative, and fun ways to express themselves.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 3zXVPU2vKXs
text:
and our use case group,
which we build inline here. And that's all you need to set
up video recording in your app. Now, I started off
this talk by saying users are looking for new,
innovative, and fun ways to express themselves. So let's look at how to
apply a simple filter, which you can build on to create
pretty much any custom experience for your users
that you can dream of. The amazing news is that
CameraX makes adding effects a breeze with the Media3 effect
connector, which is currently in alpha. Kristina will go over more
built-in Media3 effects later. But for now, let's add a
simple black-and-white filter. When the effect
should be applied, you can create a Media3 effect,
passing in the Android context, the use case targets
this effect will be applied to using a bitwise
OR, an executor, and an error listener. Then you set the
list of effects. In our case, it will just be the
built-in RGB filters grayscale filter effect. Then just add the
effect to the use case group builder we created in the
last section, and we're done. We've applied an effect to the
preview and the final video. For my last trick, I'm
going to really show off how all of the pieces of
CameraX can come together to great effect. [CANNED LAUGHTER] Thank you. Thank you. This effect will allow me to
record what's in front of me, and it will also capture
a selfie cutout of me as if I were standing in
front of a green screen. We'll start by using
CameraX's concurrent camera feature to get two camera
streams simultaneously. So we have our original use
case group builder, like before. But we'll create a secondary
use case group builder to use the image
analysis use case. We'll use an image
analysis builder for this. The back pressure
strategy is used to handle frames when the
application's analysis pipeline can't keep up
with CameraX's frame rate requirements. In our case, we're only
interested in the latest frame. So if CameraX receives a new
image before the application finishes processing,
the new image

title: Google I/O 2025 – 3zXVPU2vKXs
text:
pipeline can't keep up
with CameraX's frame rate requirements. In our case, we're only
interested in the latest frame. So if CameraX receives a new
image before the application finishes processing,
the new image is saved to the same buffer,
overwriting the previous image. Then we set the
analyzer that will do the processing of the frames. We'll define the selfie
segmentation analyzer in the next section. Then we create a secondary
use case group builder and add the image
analysis use case. Now we'll create two
single-camera configs to be combined in our
concurrent camera setup. We've selected
the primary camera to be a back camera and
the secondary camera to be a front camera. See the code in Socialite to
see exactly how we did that. Then we'll pass in our two
use case groups like so. Finally, we specify the
lifecycle owner for each config. Then we need to update the
call to bindToLifecycle to pass in our list of
single-camera configs. CameraX will handle setting up
the multiple camera streams. Next, we need to set
up our image analyzer to do selfie segmentation. We can leverage ML Kit's
selfie segmentation API for this, which can separate
people in the foreground from the background of an image. Going back to our
architecture diagram, we can create a bitmap from
each frame that comes out of our image analysis,
pass that into ML Kit, and get a grid of probabilities
that each Pixel is in the foreground, which
we can turn into a mask to remove the background. Here's the implementation of the
selfie segmentation analyzer. Read more about
the options you can set in the selfie
segmentation with ML Kit on Android guide linked in
this video's description. Inside the analyze method, we
can get the bitmap and the mask to do the green screen effect. The bitmap can be
created directly from the image we get off
of the provided image proxy. For the mask, we
can use the results of ML Kit in this
success listener. I won't go into the details
of this success listener here, so check Socialite
for the full code.

text:
from the image we get off
of the provided image proxy. For the mask, we
can use the results of ML Kit in this
success listener. I won't go into the details
of this success listener here, so check Socialite
for the full code. We're almost ready to
see the final result. We can take the
bitmap and the mask we generated in the last
section and draw the result on the original video
using an overlay effect. Similar to the Media3
effect we made earlier, we'll use CameraX's
overlay effect for drawing an overlay on
top of the camera frames. Then we set the
onDrawListener callback to draw on the overlay Canvas. Check if the mask and
bitmap is null first because we need them to proceed. Then we use this line to
clear the previous drawing on the overlay Canvas. We'll set up a Paint object to
handle the masking appropriately and, if any translations
or rotations are needed, apply those with a
transformation matrix. Then we're ready to
draw the selfie overlay. First draw the bitmap which
has all of the Pixel data. Then draw the mask on
top of it, like so. All that's left is to add
the green screen effect to our original use
case builder, which has the preview and video
capture use cases on it. So you can see my
segmented selfie being drawn on the preview
and the output video. With only my selfie
being drawn, it frees up a lot more of the
main content to be visible. And what a great view. When implementing your
own effects in your app. Just remember that everything
that applies to the camera has to happen in real time. A general rule of
thumb is that you have 30 milliseconds between
frames if your video is running at 30 frames per second. For higher frame rates, you
would have even less time. But there's good news,
because next, you'll see how to apply effects
to already existing videos where there are no
real-time constraints. Over to you, Kristina. KRISTINA SIMAKOVA: It sounds
like it's Media3 editing turn in the spotlight. Now, what happens after
the recording stops? How do we take that
footage and transform it
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 3zXVPU2vKXs
text:
where there are no
real-time constraints. Over to you, Kristina. KRISTINA SIMAKOVA: It sounds
like it's Media3 editing turn in the spotlight. Now, what happens after
the recording stops? How do we take that
footage and transform it into something extra compelling? That's where Media3 editing
capabilities come into play. Media3 provides a set
of APIs that allow you to edit media content. These APIs can help developers
with a range of capabilities. Effects is where the
creative magic happens. Media3 provides a powerful
and highly customizable APIs to create effects that
you can apply to your video. Preview is where you can
see your edits in real time. And once you are
satisfied with your edits, the export function allows
you to render the final video. We will not be covering
the export APIs in this presentation. Please check Media3 developer
guide for more details. Now let's focus on the heart of
video creation, Media3 effects. Media3 offers two approaches
to applying effects. Built-in effects are designed
for efficiency and ease of use. They handle common
video processing tasks, like scaling, rotation,
and lookup tables. Custom effects are more
advanced and specialized. This approach provides
incredible flexibility and control. But does require
OpenGL knowledge. Now let's begin with
built-in effects. These are the essential tools
of Media3 editing capabilities. Let's see a practical example
of applying a built in effect. On the left, you can see a video
clip with an RGB adjustment effect applied. And here on the
right, you can see the code snippet that achieves
this effect using Media3. We start by creating an edited
media item from our video URI. Then we use the
setEffects method, passing in an effects object. Within this object,
we specify that we want to apply an RGB
adjustment effect. This simple code is
all it takes to enhance the visual appeal of our
video using built-in effects. For a full list of
built-in effects, follow the short link
presented on the screen. Now let's move on
to custom effects.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 3zXVPU2vKXs
text:
This simple code is
all it takes to enhance the visual appeal of our
video using built-in effects. For a full list of
built-in effects, follow the short link
presented on the screen. Now let's move on
to custom effects. With custom effects, you can
unlock the full potential of media tree,
allowing for truly unique and specialized
video manipulations. When working with custom
effects in Media3, you will primarily interact with
GL effect and GL shader program. As you can see from the
interface definition from Media3's source code,
GL effect acts as a factory. The drawFrame method is the
core of your custom effect's rendering logic. It's invoked for each video
frame receiving the OpenGL texture containing the output
from the previous effects stage and the presentation time. Crucially, base
GL shader program handles texture and
frame buffer management, allowing you to focus
solely on the rendering logic within drawFrame. Let's illustrate how
to apply a custom effect with a practical
example, a vignette. On the left, you
can see a video clip with this effect applied,
creating a darkened border around the edges. And here on the right,
we define a GL effect using a lambda expression. This lambda returns an
instance of our custom shader program, which we have
created to apply the vignette. This demonstrates
how GL effect allows you to encapsulate your
custom OpenGL shader programs, making them easy to
apply within your Media3 editing pipeline. Now, for a more advanced and
visually striking example, let's explore style transfer. To create a style
transfer effect, we are leveraging the power of
LiteRT, Google's runtime library for running machine-learning
models on device. On the right, you can
see the artistic style we want to apply to our
frames and the result-- a video clip with a
style transfer effect applied to the left
part of the video, transforming its
visual appearance. By combining Media3 video
processing capabilities with LiteRT's
machine-learning power, we can achieve complex
and visually stunning
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 3zXVPU2vKXs
text:
applied to the left
part of the video, transforming its
visual appearance. By combining Media3 video
processing capabilities with LiteRT's
machine-learning power, we can achieve complex
and visually stunning effects like this. Now let's look at the
specific pixel processing for our style transfer effect. We have created
StyleTransferEffect class that implements bytebuffer
GL effect processor. To ensure smooth and
efficient video processing, we perform the style transfer
operations asynchronously. This is handled by
process image method within our
StyleTransferEffect class. As you can see, process
image takes an image object and presentation time as input. It returns a
ListenableFuture, indicating that the processing will
occur asynchronously and will eventually produce a
bitmap representing the style frame. After the asynchronous style
transfer processing is complete and the effects pipeline is
ready to render the frame, the finished processing
and blend method is called. And with that, we have explored
the power of Media3 custom effects and how we can combine
it with tools like LiteRT to create powerful
video effects. Now let's talk about the vital
step of previewing our edits. To simplify this process, Media3
introduces the CompositionPlayer API. CompositionPlayer is designed
to streamline the implementation of previewing media edits. It allows your app to play a
composition of media assets, effectively rendering all
applied audio and video effects in real time. This means you can see
exactly how your edits will look before exporting
the final video. It's important to note that
CompositionPlayer is currently in early preview and represents
the first introduction of this API. We are actively working
on its development, so expect further
enhancements and refinements in future releases. CompositionPlayer
offers real-time preview of complex media
edits, allowing users to fine-tune their work
before finalizing it. It is built upon the familiar
Media3 player interface, and you can use the
same composition
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 3zXVPU2vKXs
text:
CompositionPlayer
offers real-time preview of complex media
edits, allowing users to fine-tune their work
before finalizing it. It is built upon the familiar
Media3 player interface, and you can use the
same composition for preview and export. This API streamlines
the editing process, allowing users to iterate
quickly and confidently, knowing that their preview
accurately reflects the final exported video. Let's look at the code sample. First, we create our
composition object, which encapsulates our edited
media assets and effects. Next, we instantiate
a composition player using the builder pattern. The crucial difference lies in
how we set the media source. Instead of using set media
items or set playlist, we use set composition to
provide our composition object to the player. Finally, we call prepare and
play to start the preview. Now let's see composition player
in action with a final preview. On the right, you will see
a video clip demonstrating the preview of a composition
with several effects applied. CompositionPlayer allows us to
preview all of these effects seamlessly, ensuring that
the final output matches our creative intent. This is the culmination of
the editing process, where we can fine-tune and confirm
that our composition is ready for export
using transform APIs. I am pretty happy with
how this video turned out. Mayuri, can you help
us with the next step? MAYURI KHINVASARA
KHABYA: Of course. Hi there. Donovan and Kristina
showed us how to capture, edit, preview
the videos with effects. Now you might want to share
and watch it with others. And that's where we can use the
Media3 ExoPlayer for playback. For more than 10 years, the
opensource ExoPlayer library, now integrated as
a part of Media3, has been a recommended solution
for everything Android playback. ExoPlayer is designed for
efficient real-time playback. It's quick and easy
to set up ExoPlayer with only a few lines of code. Generally, you'll need
to provide a media item or a list of media
items that references a media
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 3zXVPU2vKXs
text:
ExoPlayer is designed for
efficient real-time playback. It's quick and easy
to set up ExoPlayer with only a few lines of code. Generally, you'll need
to provide a media item or a list of media
items that references a media source, like a URL, local file,
or content URI, and prepare it for playback. Is there anything that stands
out to you in this code snippet? We're thrilled to
announce the new Compose artifact for ExoPlayer-- introducing the PlayerSurface. Check out our documentation
for more details. Please note that this API is
currently in early preview and represents its
initial release. We are actively developing it,
so expect further enhancements in future releases. ExoPlayer capabilities
extend even further. We are very excited to
share that you can directly set effects within
ExoPlayer itself. It provides you with
a set video effects API, a method for applying
real-time playback effects, usually on the entire video. How awesome is that? You apply these effects
to ExoPlayer instance using the setVideoEffects
API method, passing a list of
effect objects. ExoPlayer takes the
currently loaded media item or the active media
item in a playlist, decodes its audio
and video streams, and then applies the
configured effects during the rendering process. The setVideoEffects
API allows you to set a list of
effect objects that will process all frames as they
are output from the player. For example, here, we
set a single effect that improves contrast to
help with accessibility. You can create
these effect objects using the respective
builder classes. If you have a playlist
of media items and you set effects
on ExoPlayer, those effects will
generally continue to apply to subsequent media
items in that playlist, unless you explicitly change
or clear the effects using setVideoEffects API again. Donovan and Kristina showed
us a bunch of Media3 effects throughout this talk so far,
and you can try them out by adding to the effects
objects in a list. And of course, as well
as applying effects
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 3zXVPU2vKXs
text:
setVideoEffects API again. Donovan and Kristina showed
us a bunch of Media3 effects throughout this talk so far,
and you can try them out by adding to the effects
objects in a list. And of course, as well
as applying effects during playback, videos
exported from CameraX and transformers are
playable with ExoPlayer too. So you've created, previewed,
and edited your videos. How do you view and share them? You might wonder, why do we have
two players to view, ExoPlayer and CompositionPlayer? Let's see when should
you use which one. In CompositionPlayer, you
need to work on a composition, whereas in ExoPlayer, you work
on a media item or a playlist. In CompositionPlayer,
effects are set on each item or composition,
whereas in ExoPlayer, effects are set onto the
player, thus will be applied on each media
item in the playlist that you are playing. Last one is the timeline-- in CompositionPlayer,
the progress is calculated based on the
duration of the set composition, whereas in ExoPlayer,
the progress bar is tied to the currently
playing media item. Wow, that was a lot of
interesting and cool stuff. The main key takeaway that we
would want you to take back is that CameraX and Media3
work seamlessly together. And we highly, highly
recommend using these to Android developers. For a quick recap, we can do
capture, analyze, preview, editing playback with all of
CameraX in Media3 effects, all seamlessly with
one cohesive solution. First, Donovan walked us through
using the Jetpack CameraX Library to capture some
footage and apply effects to the video in real time,
such as the grayscale effect or a green screen
effect that uses both the front-facing and
the back-facing cameras of the device at the same time. Next, Kristina showed us how to
use Jetpack Media3 transformers to add some extra magic to the
video with built-in effects, like the RGB filter, custom
effects like a vignette, and even more advanced frame
processing, like style transfer, powered by LiteRT. And lastly, after
previewing and exporting

to add some extra magic to the
video with built-in effects, like the RGB filter, custom
effects like a vignette, and even more advanced frame
processing, like style transfer, powered by LiteRT. And lastly, after
previewing and exporting our final edited
video, we saw how to use the Jetpack
Media3 ExoPlayer to play the videos in
our Compose first UI and apply a final
round of effects, like adjusting the
contrast if desired. And look at what we were able
to create, capture, edit, preview, playback, all in one. They all come together
to create a pretty cool end-to-end journey, isn't it? That's also the key
takeaway that we want to give you for this talk. CameraX and Media3 work
seamlessly together to help you build amazing
apps very easily on Android. Delighted? We are too. How do you go on building
these experiences? So here are our top
three recommendations that you could go back and
do after you we sign off from this talk. First, check out
our hero sample app, socialite, on GitHub
to see all the features we talked about today and
many more in the context of a social media app. Second, visit the
Media3 GitHub repo and explore both the composition
demo and the effects demo app for more examples on how to
use the effects framework. There are some pretty
cool demos right there. Last, check out our
guides and documentations in the Camera and
Media Developer Center for more tips on how to
supercharge your Android apps. We can't wait to see all
the cool and amazing effects you build in your Android
apps to delight your users. Thank you for tuning in. Bye-bye. [MUSIC PLAYING]
